title: Runny nose versus nasal congestion
summary: A runny nose is excess discharge; congestion is swelling that narrows the nasal passages. Saline is low-risk for both, while decongestants mainly target swelling and should be used briefly and selectively.
evidenceSnapshot: Saline may modestly improve acute upper-respiratory symptoms. Short-term decongestants can improve adult congestion, but oral phenylephrine has poor efficacy evidence.

interventions.name: Saline spray or drops
interventions.finding: Can moisturise dry passages and loosen mucus with minimal systemic risk.
interventions.practical: Use as often as the product directs. Drops are useful for infants before feeds or sleep.
interventions.mechanism: Changes mucus consistency and mechanically clears secretions.
interventions.cautions: Keep applicators clean and do not share bottles.

interventions.name: Larger-volume saline irrigation
interventions.finding: May improve congestion and discharge, but acute-cold trials are small and at risk of bias.
interventions.practical: Use premixed sachets or an isotonic recipe supplied by the device manufacturer; use only distilled, sterile, or boiled-and-cooled water.
interventions.mechanism: Mechanical flushing removes mucus and irritants.
interventions.cautions: Incorrect water or device hygiene can cause rare severe infections.

interventions.name: Oral pseudoephedrine
interventions.finding: Can provide short-term congestion relief in adults and older adolescents.
interventions.practical: In Australia it is pharmacist-only or prescription-only depending on formulation; ask the pharmacist whether it is suitable.
interventions.mechanism: Systemic vasoconstriction reduces swollen nasal blood vessels.
interventions.cautions: Can raise heart rate or blood pressure and worsen insomnia, anxiety, urinary retention or glaucoma.

interventions.name: Oral phenylephrine
interventions.finding: The US FDA's extensive review concluded that standard oral doses are not effective as nasal decongestants, although Australian products may still contain it.
interventions.practical: Read the active ingredients; do not assume a 'PE' product is equivalent to pseudoephedrine.
interventions.mechanism: Oral bioavailability is too low to produce reliable decongestion at marketed doses.
interventions.cautions: Still has sympathomimetic cautions despite doubtful efficacy.

interventions.name: Topical decongestant sprays
interventions.finding: Oxymetazoline or xylometazoline can rapidly reduce congestion for a few nights.
interventions.practical: Use only for the shortest label-approved period, commonly no more than 3 days unless a clinician advises otherwise.
interventions.cautions: Longer use can cause rebound congestion; keep away from young children unless specifically advised.

practicalSteps: Blow gently, one nostril at a time.
Use saline before feeds and sleep in infants.
Treat allergy differently from a viral cold; persistent itch/sneezing may need antihistamine or steroid nasal-spray advice.
Sleep with the head slightly elevated if congestion is worse lying flat.
avoidOrCare: One-sided persistent discharge, especially foul-smelling in a child, may indicate a foreign body.
Severe facial pain, eye swelling, vision change or severe headache needs urgent review.
Do not use medicated nasal sprays for longer than directed.

myths.claim: Green mucus proves a bacterial sinus infection.
myths.reality: Mucus often changes colour as immune cells accumulate; colour alone does not identify bacterial infection.
Higher or repeated doses increase cardiovascular and nervous-system risks without guaranteeing better relief.

redFlags: Eye swelling, vision change or intense facial pain
Fluid or blood after head injury
One-sided blockage that does not improve
Symptoms lasting over 10 days without improvement or worsening after initial recovery
unsettled: The magnitude of benefit from saline irrigation in uncomplicated acute colds remains uncertain.
